vulnerability.title: Add nodev Option to /boot
vulnerability.description: The nodev mount option can be used to prevent device files from being created in /boot. Legitimate character and block devices should exist only in the /dev directory on the root partition or within chroot jails built for system services. Add the nodev option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /boot.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ); then function perform_remediation { # the mount point /boot has to be defined in /etc/fstab # before this remediation can be executed. In case it is not defined, the # remediation aborts and no changes regarding the mount point are done. mount_point_match_regexp="$(printf "^[[:space:]]*[^#].*[[:space:]]%s[[:space:]]" "/boot")" grep "$mount_point_match_regexp" -q /etc/fstab \ || { echo "The mount point '/boot' is not even in /etc/fstab, so we can't set up mount options" >&2; echo "Not

triage_decision.reason: Adding the 'nodev' mount option to the /boot entry in /etc/fstab is a simple configuration edit that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. The change is reversible by restoring the original fstab line and only requires a reboot (or remount) to take effect. While an incorrect fstab syntax could prevent the system from booting, the risk is low and can be mitigated by backing up the file before editing.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential boot failure if /etc/fstab syntax is malformed; Requires reboot or remount to apply

llm_verdict.message: Glad to hear the remediation was successful! If you need any further verification, additional hardening, or assistance with other findings, just let me know. I'm here to help.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly adds the nodev option to the /boot entry, backs up /etc/fstab, validates the syntax and provides a rollback path. The overall approach satisfies the security requirement. However, the sed command used is overly complex and could be fragile; a simpler, more maintainable method (e.g., awk or a dedicated fstab editor) would reduce the risk of corrupting the file.
concerns: The in‑place sed expression is complex and may not match all possible fstab formats, potentially corrupting the line.
No explicit check for an existing nodev option, which could lead to duplicate entries.
The script assumes /boot is a separate filesystem without confirming it via findmnt.
If the sed edit fails, the system could be left with an invalid /etc/fstab, preventing boot.
suggested_improvements: Use a line‑oriented parser (e.g., awk) to safely modify only the fourth column of the /boot entry.
Add a pre‑edit check to see if nodev is already present and skip the append if so.
Verify that /boot is indeed a mounted filesystem (findmnt /boot) before editing the fstab line.
After editing, run `findmnt --verify` in addition to `mount -a` to catch syntax errors early.
Consider applying the change immediately with `systemd-remount-fs.service` after validation, reducing the need for a reboot.
